title: The Impact of Supervision on Bank Performance
abstract: We explore the impact of supervision on the riskiness, profitability, and growth of U.S. banks. Using data on supervisors' time use, we demonstrate that the top-ranked banks by size within a supervisory district receive more attention from supervisors, even after controlling for size, complexity, risk, and other characteristics. Using a matched sample approach, we find that these top-ranked banks that receive more supervisory attention hold less risky loan portfolios, are less volatile, and are less sensitive to industry downturns, but do not have lower growth or profitability. Our results underscore the distinct role of supervision in mitigating banking sector risk.

article.introduction:
Supervision and regulation arecritical tools for the promotion of stability and soundness in the financial sector. Despite supervision's key role, it is rarely examined separately from regulation and relatively little is known about the distinct impact of supervisory efforts. In this paper, we exploit new supervisory data and develop a novel identification strategy to estimate the impact of greater supervision on bank risk-taking and performance. We find that more supervision adds value over and above the effects of regulation. In particular, banks that receive more supervisory attention have less risky loan portfolios, are less volatile, and are less sensitive to industry downturns, but do not exhibit lower growth or profitability.
While prior work examines specific supervisory actions such as examinations or enforcement actions, little is known about the effectiveness of supervisors' more general efforts to promote sound risk management. For example, supervisors meet frequently with bank management, discussing both specific issues related to activities at the bank and more general perspectives on the industry's environment and outlook. These conversations, along with analysis of internal firm reports and external data, allow supervisors to understand a bank's risks and procedures and to put these in context relative to the industry. By focusing on a broad concept of supervisory attention, our analysis captures the breadth of supervisory efforts without restriction to a single supervisory program.
Reflecting the potential externalities of bank failures, supervisors seek to reduce failure risk relative to what banks themselves might otherwise choose. Our analysis sheds light on whether supervisors achieve this objective and at what cost to financial intermediation. Because actual bank failures are infrequent, especially among the largest banks that receive the most supervisory attention, we rely on a variety of risk measures, including measures of lending riskiness (e.g., loan losses and reserve practices), measures of earnings risk (e.g., earnings volatility), as well as measures of market riskiness (e.g., return volatility).
The key empirical hurdle to identifying the impact of supervision is that larger and riskier firms receive more supervisory attention. To address this challenge, we exploit the structure of supervisory responsibilities within the Federal Reserve System, under which bank holding companies (BHCs or “banks”) are geographically assigned to 1 of 12 Federal Reserve districts. We hypothesize that within each district, the largest institutions receive more supervisory attention, ceteris paribus, than institutions that are not among the largest. We validate this hypothesis using proprietary Federal Reserve data by showing that examiners spend more time at the largest firms in a district, even when controlling for firm characteristics such as size, market share, complexity, and supervisory rating.
We match top-ranked BHCs in each Federal Reserve district by size, deposit market share, organizational complexity, types of banking subsidiaries, diversity of lending activities, and other characteristics to non-top-ranked banks in other districts. Doing so allows us to construct a sample of banks that have varying ranks in their Federal Reserve districts but that are otherwise similar. The matching procedure is designed to control for differences across banks that might be correlated with rank and performance, but excludes outcome variables that might be directly influenced by supervision. Thus, by comparing outcomes of banks that are among the largest in a district to otherwise similar banks that are not among the largest in other districts, we are able to interpret differences in outcomes as reflecting the impact of greater supervisory attention.
Our findings suggest that enhanced supervisory attention is associated with lower risk. Accounting earnings and market returns are less volatile for the largest banks in a district than for otherwise similar BHCs. Moreover, top-size-ranked banks on average have 15% to 25% lower nonperforming loan (NPL) ratios than peers not among the largest in their district, and they appear to engage in more conservative loan loss reserve practices. The reduction in risk is greater during downturns, precisely when the externalities associated with failure are costlier. With respect to volatility, our findings suggest that a 20% increase in supervisory hours is associated with a 9% to 10% decrease in risk as measured by the volatility of earnings, a reduction in distance to default equivalent to 150 to 200 bps of additional capital.
Importantly, while top-ranked BHCs appear to be less risky, they do not exhibit lower profitability, nor do they exhibit significantly slower loan or asset growth. Specifically, we find no meaningful differences in market or accounting measures of profitability, including return on assets (ROAs) and excess stock price returns, between top-ranked BHCs and their matches. Further, the market Sharpe ratio of top-ranked BHCs is similar to that of BHCs not among the top-size-ranked firms. These findings are consistent with additional supervisory attention resulting in lower risk exposure but having a positive-to-neutral impact on the risk-adjusted performance. Our findings therefore suggest that supervision meaningfully improves social welfare by decreasing the risk of failure among large banks without significantly reducing banks' intermediation activities.
Our identifying assumption is that being among the largest firms in a Federal Reserve district is not associated with other unobserved factors that also affect bank performance. We take a number of steps to account for potentially confounding factors. For example, differences in risk across districts might drive our results. To account for unobserved district-level differences, we use a larger matched sample and include district-quarter fixed effects. Differences in competitiveness and franchise value may also play a role. To address this, we consider additional matching criteria, including recent firm performance and proxies for franchise value such as market-to-book. Our conclusions are robust to using these alternative specifications.
Overall, our results suggest that increased supervisory attention results in lower risk without significantly reduced performance. However, our empirical approach does not shed light on the specific mechanisms by which supervision achieves these outcomes. One possibility is that supervision helps mitigate principal-agent problems within the firm. In particular, enhanced supervision may result in more weight being given to concerns of risk managers, resulting in more disciplined risk-taking. Supervisors may thus improve a bank's risk culture, by increasing attention to risk as a balance against a focus on short-term profitability. Supervisory requests for risk and activity information may also lead banks to invest in data and technology systems that allow them to manage their business more efficiently over the long run. Finally, because supervisors oversee many banks, they may transmit knowledge of best practices in the industry when they provide feedback to banks about their risk management practices.
Previous work on the supervision and regulation of banks focuses largely on the impact of regulation, though the distinction between supervision and regulation is not always clearly recognized or articulated.1Fewer papers focus specifically on supervision. Of these that do, several papers examine the information content of supervisory ratings (Cargill (1989), Cole and Gunther (1995), Hirtle and Lopez (1999), Berger, Davies, and Flannery (2000)) and examinations (Berger and Davies (1998)) but not the impact of supervision on bank outcomes. Several other papers examine whether supervisory standards—how tough examiners are in assessing risk at banks—affect loan origination and loan growth (Peek and Rosengren (1995), Swindle (1995), Krainer and Lopez (2009), Kiser, Prager, and Scott (2012), Bassett, Lee, and Spiller (2015), Bassett and Marsh (2017)), with most finding that tougher supervisory standards are associated with slower loan growth and/or higher origination standards. Others examine the use of enforcement actions on bank sector risk (e.g., Delis and Staikouras (2011)). We contribute to this literature by estimating the impact of supervisory attention that encompasses the breadth of supervisory interactions with banks.
We further contribute to the literature by developing a new identification strategy based on the structure of supervision at the Federal Reserve. Plausibly exogenous variation in supervisory attention allows us to go beyond correlations to identify the impact of supervision. Our paper is similar in spirit to recent work that examines state versus federal banking supervisors, including Agarwal et al. (2014), who find persistent differences between state and federal banking supervisors in the rating of commercial banks, and Rezende (2016), who finds that banks switching between national and state banking charters typically receive an upgraded rating from their new supervisor. In more closely related work, Rezende and Wu (2014) employ a regression discontinuity approach to look at a sample of U.S. banks and find that more frequent mandated examinations are associated with increased profitability and lower loan losses. Recent work investigating the impact of supervisory office closures (Gopalan, Kalda, and Manela (2017), Hagendorff, Lim, and Armitage (2017)) suggests that the removal of nearby supervisors results in greater risk and lower profitability. To the extent these closures are a proxy for a reduction in supervisory attentiveness, these latter studies corroborate our findings. In comparison to these papers, we focus on supervisory attention in general rather than a specific activity like examinations, we demonstrate variation in attention using novel data on the time that supervisors spend at institutions, and we consider the impact on relatively large firms.
While we consider several alternative explanations for our results, it is difficult to capture constructs such as franchise value and market competition. To the extent that our matching criteria fail to capture these factors, we cannot rule out the possibility that these forces explain part of the reduction in risk that we attribute to supervisory attention. In addition, we cannot rule out other possible sources of unobserved heterogeneity. For example, we do not observe the quality of supervision. While unlikely, if supervisory hours are more productive in districts with smaller banks, then supervisory quality could explain some of our findings.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the role of prudential supervision within the Federal Reserve and discusses the relation between supervisory attention and bank outcomes. SectionIIoutlines our identification strategy, describes the supervisory hours data, and summarizes differences in supervisory hours for the largest firms in a district. SectionIIIoutlines our empirical methodology for assessing the impact of supervision, including identifying a matched sample of BHCs. SectionIVpresents our main empirical results and SectionVconsiders alternative empirical specifications. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Prudential Supervision:
The overarching objective of supervision is to identify and remediate conditions that could threaten banks' immediate health or long-term viability. Toward that end, prudential supervision encompasses a range of supervisory activities that support both traditional efforts to ensure compliance with law and regulation as well as more modern “prudential” efforts to monitor for unsafe or unsound business practices. Federal Reserve supervisory expectations for certain activities, risk management, or control procedures are often articulated in Supervision and Regulation Letters (SR Letters) published by the Board of Governors (Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (2017c)).
For many years, supervisors made their assessments based on a point-in-time analysis of a bank, typically once a year, in the form of an annual examination. This process was inherently backward looking because it focused on reviewing the quality of a bank's loans and other assets as of the exam date (Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (1997)). Beginning in the early 1990s, there was a transition toward a more holistic forward-looking approach to supervision, as supervisors sought to make institutions more robust in the face of rapid financial innovation (Mishkin (2001)). For example, in 1995, the Federal Reserve and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) formally announced that they would be assessing banks' risk management practices. Today a large share of the interactions between bankers and supervisors, particularly for large banks, centers on risk management, risk modeling, and governance (Goldsmith-Pinkham, Hirtle, and Lucca (2016)).
Such forward-looking assessments of risk management and internal controls involve both quantitative analysis and qualitative evaluations. These qualitative assessments are grounded in knowledge of industry standards and evolve over time to adapt to financial innovation. Hence, modern supervisory activities rely on both soft and hard information and are inherently difficult to quantify, both in terms of the work that supervisors actually do and in terms of supervisory outcomes related to banks' internal risk management, controls, and governance. Our empirical strategy is designed to capture not only the impact of traditional, exam-based interactions, but also the influence of these more difficult to quantify interactions that are central to modern supervisory efforts.
If a supervisory assessment identifies shortcomings, supervisors possess a range of responses to induce the bank to rectify the problems, from formal enforcement actions and ratings downgrades, which can constrain bank activities, to more subtle warnings that work via moral suasion. Supervisors assign confidential supervisory ratings (“1” indicates the lowest level of supervisory concern, “5” indicates the highest) and issue supervisory actions that direct the bank to remediate unsafe or unsound practices or conditions. Supervisory actions include matters requiring attention (MRAs), matters requiring immediate attention (MRIAs), other informal enforcement actions such as memoranda of understanding (MOUs), as well as written agreements, cease and desist orders, and fines. MRAs and MRIAs are the most common supervisory actions. In general, informal actions such as MRAs and MRIAs are not publicly disclosed, while formal enforcement actions are disclosed by the Federal Reserve Board.
Supervisory actions describe the specific concern in detail and generally require a time table for remediation. Banks subject to an action typically develop a remediation plan, which is subject to approval by supervisors, who then track progress against the plan. Failure to address concerns raised by supervisors in a timely fashion can result in escalation of the enforcement action, fines (“civil money penalties”), restrictions on growth, or limits on shareholder payouts. Thus, supervisory actions can have real consequences for the business activities of a bank that fails to comply with its supervisor's directives.2
Given externalities from bank failures, supervisors prefer a lower risk of failure or distress than do bank managers, who do not internalize the costs of a disruption in intermediation services that include reduced credit supply and fire-sale-related asset price declines. To the extent that supervision is successful, banks that are subject to more intense supervision should take less risk and follow more conservative risk management practices. First, greater supervisory focus on risk management and governance is likely to increase the influence of a bank's risk managers, whose perspectives may be more aligned with supervisors than business area heads, and thus, help resolve agency problems within the bank. In addition, greater supervisory attention is likely to foster a stronger risk culture at banks by increasing focus on risk exposure as a balance against incentives to generate short-run profits. Chaly et al. (2017), for instance, argue that the impact of supervisors on banks' “cultural capital” is an important channel for enhancing resiliency and fostering stable provision of financial services. We therefore predict that, all else equal, greater supervisory efforts result in less risky institutions.
Of course, it is possible that more intense supervision might not result in less risky banking institutions. For example, supervisors could fail to achieve their objectives due to resource constraints that make it difficult to work effectively at large and complex institutions, even with increased attention on those firms, or a bank may have influence over its supervisors, resulting in greater forbearance and, in turn, more risk. Thus, the effect of supervision on bank risk is an empirical question.
Turning to the effect of supervisory attention on banks' financial performance, we predict that compliance costs reduce banks' profitability and growth. For instance, cross-country analysis suggests that supervision can reduce bank efficiency (e.g., Barth et al. (2013)), and supervisory concerns about risk management could result in banks' making technology investments with large up-front costs, thereby depressing near-term profits. Lower profitability constrains access to capital that is necessary for growth. Also, if supervisors increase banks' sensitivity to risk, banks may have fewer lending opportunities resulting in slower growth. Consistent with this view, prior empirical literature suggests that tougher supervisory standards are associated with slower loan growth (e.g., Peek and Rosengren (1995)).
However, the performance of more intensely supervised banks could be higher on a risk-adjusted basis. For instance, investments in superior technology and information systems may allow banks to make better risk-return decisions or identify operational inefficiencies. In this spirit, Tarullo (2016) argues that supervisory expectations of the Federal Reserve's Comprehensive Capital Analysis and Review (CCAR) program have resulted in large banks making significant improvements to their information and risk management systems. Similarly, Lam (1999) documents that according to a senior risk manager, consolidated risk management systems can allow banks to make better business decisions by incorporating risk-return considerations in pricing, product development, and relationship management. Since supervisors interact with a range of banks, they may also transmit best practices in risk management and controls in feedback to banks about where they stand, helping spread best practices across the industry. In sum, the effect of supervision on banks' financial performance is also ultimately an empirical question.
article.II. Identification Strategy:
Our empirical analysis seeks to examine the impact of more intensive banking supervision. In particular, we wish to provide evidence on whether supervision decreases bank risk and on whether it reduces bank performance as measured by profitability and growth.
The primary empirical challenge in identifying the impact of supervision is that supervisory attention is endogenously related to current and expected bank performance: supervisors focus on poorly performing, risky BHCs. Supervisors also expend more resources on large, complex institutions that pose a greater threat to financial stability.
To identify plausibly exogenous variation in supervisory attention, we exploit the geographic assignment of BHCs to Federal Reserve districts. Within the Federal Reserve, the Board of Governors has authority and responsibility for supervision of financial institutions, and the supervisory activities of the Reserve Banks are conducted under delegated authority from the Board. Under this delegated authority, day-to-day oversight of BHCs is conducted by the 12 regional Reserve Banks, which employ dedicated supervisory teams responsible for the BHCs located in their respective districts. The location of the 12 Reserve Banks and the boundaries of their districts were determined pursuant to the Federal Reserve Act of 1913 and reflect the various regions' importance as banking centers in 1913 (Ghizoni (2013)).
Since that time, the district boundaries have remained the same despite significant changes in the distribution of BHCs and the rise and fall of U.S. banking centers. Consequently, both the number and size of BHCs vary considerably across districts. TableIreports the size of the largest BHCs in each of the 12 districts as of December 2014, along with the median asset size of BHCs with assets above $500 million.3The table contains consolidated information on the highest level BHC in each firm's corporate structure. The number of BHCs over $500 million ranges from a low of 57 in the Fourth District (Cleveland) to a high of 157 in the Seventh (Chicago). The size of the largest BHCs in a district also varies considerably, with the assets of the largest BHC in the Second District (New York) amounting to $2.6 trillion and those of the largest BHC in the Eighth District (St. Louis) totaling $26 billion.
Each of the 12 Federal Reserve Banks supervises the BHCs headquartered in its geographic district, hosting dedicated supervisory teams responsible for these firms. The Reserve Banks have their own managerial hierarchy, which makes hiring, performance assessment, and staff allocation decisions subject to general oversight of its budget and activities by the Board of Governors (Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (2017a)). These staffing decisions include the number of supervisory staff and their educational and professional backgrounds, as well as the way that supervisory staff are organized to carry out their oversight objectives.
Some supervisory activities for BHCs are coordinated at the Federal Reserve System level via committees composed of senior supervisors from the Reserve Banks and staff at the Board of Governors. Since 2012, supervision of the very largest and most complex BHCs has been coordinated across Reserve Banks and the Board of Governors through the Large Institution Supervision Coordinating Committee (LISCC) program (Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (2017b)). As we describe below, these firms drop out of our sample through the matching process—the nature of our analysis is cross-district and typically excludes the largest BHCs. The impact of system-wide programs is therefore minimal in this study.
Given the district-level management of supervision, we posit that all else equal the largest BHCs in a district receive more supervisory attention. First, attention constraints on senior managers can lead them to prioritize the most important BHCs in their district (i.e., Miller's Law4). This explanation is motivated by research span of control and the allocation of managerial attention, such as Bolton and Dewatripont (1994), Garicano (2000), Geanakoplos and Milgrom (1991), and Radner (1993). In our context, district leaders are subject to cognitive constraints, and thus, they focus on the largest firms within their geographic area of responsibility (i.e., their span of control). Second, supervisors may be particularly concerned about large bank failures because such failures pose outsized negative externalities on the regional economy. As a result, supervisors could allocate their time in a way that ensures the soundness of the largest institutions under the Reserve Bank's purview.
Our analysis is ultimately indifferent as to which of these two mechanisms results in greater supervisory attention. Importantly, for our purposes, however, both mechanisms suggest that the largest BHCs within a district will receive greater attention than otherwise similar BHCs for reasons unrelated to risk or performance.
To provide evidence on the impact of supervision, we begin by constructing a simple measure of supervisory scrutiny—the total number of hours spent by Federal Reserve supervisors directly examining a particular BHC and its subsidiaries. We construct this measure on a quarterly basis using confidential Federal Reserve System managerial data on the time use of supervisors at the Reserve Banks. Supervision personnel are required to self-report time use. As part of this reporting, they are instructed to indicate the number of hours spent directly supervising a particular institution (as opposed to broadly contributing to the supervision of a portfolio of banks or participating in other activities). The data cover supervisory staff at all 12 Federal Reserve districts over the period 2006 to 2014. We do not capture hours not allocated to specific firms, nor do we include hours spent by Board of Governors supervisory staff. To the extent that Board hours substitute for supervisory hours by the Federal Reserve Banks, their exclusion should attenuate our results.
We note that many BHC-quarters do not have directly reported hours. If the institution has never received directly reported hours, we assume that the firm was supervised by a team that oversees a portfolio of firms, in which case supervisors would not directly record time use at the institution, and thus, we leave this field as missing. If, however, a BHC has reported hours in a prior quarter, we assume that the missing reported hours are zero.5Because reporting conventions can vary, in some cases making it difficult to compare hours across Federal Reserve districts or over time,6we account for this variation when we analyze how hours vary with the size rank of a BHC.
Next, we match the time-use data to the consolidated financials of the parent BHC. The financials are based on FR Y-9C reports submitted to the Federal Reserve quarterly. We start with the sample of firms with above-median total assets each quarter, as firms below this threshold rarely receive reported hours and our attention measure focuses on the largest firms with reported hours. Using this sample of BHCs, we calculate the asset size rank of each BHC within its geographic Federal Reserve district.
Our approach to identifying the impact of supervision is to compare outcomes of BHCs that are similar except for their assigned Federal Reserve district and their size rank within that district. Thus, after calculating ranks, we exclude a small number of atypical institutions (e.g., payment processors, credit card banks, automobile lenders) that are difficult to compare to firms of similar size due to the relatively small number of such firms.7This filter results in the loss of 8% of the BHC-quarters from 2006 to 2014. We also exclude BHCs with foreign parents (2.5% of the firm-quarters) and BHCs that are assigned to a supervisor that is distinct from their geographic district (1.5%), as these characteristics can influence both reported hours and supervisory attention. Our final sample comprises 14,909 BHC-quarters over the 2006 to 2014 period.
We run our benchmark using variables intended to capture the various characteristics of BHCs that should attract supervisory attention. In particular, it is important that we include characteristics that are also correlated with rank to minimize concerns of omitted variable bias and to allow us to interpret rank as an exogenous indicator of supervisory attention. To this end, the first set of bank characteristics that we control for are size and complexity. Larger, more complex institutions require more resources to supervise and present a greater threat to financial stability if adversely shocked. We use the log of assets to capture size in a way that reflects that the marginal need for supervision is decreasing with scale.8In an alternative specification, we include an optimized fractional polynomial in assets to further generalize the relationship between supervisory hours and assets. We distinguish complexity from size by using the log of the number of legal entities controlled by the BHC.9
Our second set of controls captures underlying bank charters using asset composition. In addition to BHCs, the Federal Reserve has supervisory responsibility over state-chartered commercial banks that are members of the Federal Reserve System (State Member Banks [SMBs]), and thus BHCs with SMB subsidiaries should receive additional Federal Reserve scrutiny. SMBs with assets greater than $10 billion are examined jointly by the Federal Reserve and state banking supervisors, while SMBs with less than $10 billion in assets rotate examinations with state supervisors (Agarwal et al. (2014)). Accordingly, we use Call Report data to construct the percent of BHC assets in SMB subsidiaries with assets greater than or equal to $10 billion, and the percent of assets in SMB subsidiaries with assets below $10 billion. We also control for the percent of BHC assets in nationally chartered banks, as these banks are supervised by the OCC. Public firms are also subject to market scrutiny, which could substitute for or complement supervisory attention, and thus, we also include an indicator that identifies public firms.
The third set of controls pertain to business activities that might merit supervisory attention. In particular, we consider the percent of assets that are loans and the percent of liabilities that are deposits to control for potential differences in the supervisory hours related to lending and deposit-taking. In addition, we control for the diversity of the asset mix using the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) of assets. The idea being that greater business complexity (as reflected by more types of assets) can require more supervisory attention.10We further account for the market power of the firm in the areas in which it operates by using the deposit market share of the BHC (e.g., James and Wier (1987)). We measure market share at the firm level by calculating the weighted sum of county-level deposit shares where the weights are the level of local deposits. Data for local deposits come from the FDIC's Summary of Deposits.
The remaining controls include bank performance and risk characteristics. In our analysis below, we consider many of these variables as outcomes of supervisory attention, but we include them here to present a comprehensive description of supervisory hours and to conduct a strict test of whether size rank affects supervisory attention. The fourth set of controls includes a bank's recent performance and riskiness. Specifically, we include the lagged four-quarter average ROA, year-over-year asset growth, Tier 1 capital ratio, percent of loans that are nonperforming, and standard deviation of ROA over the last eight quarters. We trim the ROA, standard deviation of ROA, and asset growth variables at the top and bottom 1% to remove extreme outliers.
Our fifth and final set of variables includes indicators for the Federal Reserve's most recent composite rating for the BHC. The indicators reflect a rating of 2, 3, 4, or 5 (least sound), where 1 is the omitted category. By conditioning on supervisory rating, we capture the perceived riskiness of the BHC based on supervisory assessments that include not only publicly available information, but also information available only to supervisors.
TableIIsummarizes the results. Column (1) reports results for our simplest model on the allocation of supervisory hours, which includes size and subsidiary bank-type controls in the presence of quarter fixed effects. As expected, supervisory hours increase with assets and the number of legal entities. Supervisory hours are also increasing in SMB assets, regardless of whether those assets are in an SMB that is rotated with state supervisors or not. We do not find evidence that national bank assets or publicly traded equity are related to supervisory hours. Note that theR2of this regression is quite high at 47%, consistent with the allocation of supervisory hours being largely explained by bank size, complexity, and type. Time fixed effects alone generate anR2of only 1%.
Progressing across columns (2) to (4), we consider the additional sets of control variables conditional on those in column (1). We consider the full set of controls in column (5). None of the business controls is statistically significant in column (2), suggesting that the broad composition of assets and liabilities is not critical to the allocation of supervisory hours. In column (3), recent ROA and asset growth are negatively correlated with supervisory hours, while the share of NPLs and the standard deviation of ROA are positively correlated with supervisory hours. Hence, firms with greater earnings or asset growth receive relatively less supervisory attention, while poorly performing or risky firms receive more attention. When we consider supervisory ratings in column (4), not surprisingly we find that BHCs judged to be risky by supervisors (ratings 3 through 5) receive two to three times as many supervisory hours as do highly rated firms. In column (5), which includes all of our proposed controls for supervisory hours, the results are largely similar, although greater asset concentration is now significantly negatively correlated with hours and some performance measures (i.e., asset growth and the standard deviation of ROA) are no longer statistically significant.
The specification in column (5) serves as our preferred empirical benchmark for the allocation of supervisory hours. As can be seen, approximately 53% of the variation in supervisory hours is explained by these variables. Also, this specification includes both publicly observable controls and supervisory ratings that are based on confidential information reported to supervisors.
For robustness, we run two additional tests. As mentioned above, the first, in column (6), includes an optimized fractional polynomial for assets rather than the log of assets. This specification allows for greater flexibility in the relationship between hours and assets. We find thatR2increases by a negligible amount to 54%. The second alternative specification, in column (7), includes district-quarter fixed effects, which allow us to make within-district comparisons. Rank may be associated with greater attention not only across districts, but also within a district conditional on the benchmark model of supervision. This specification allows us to account for changes in hours reporting patterns by district over time.11
We use the above benchmark to test whether the top-ranked BHCs in a Federal Reserve district do indeed receive more supervisory attention. Based on the benchmark model in column (5), we estimate and plot the value of size-rank dummies in Figure1, Panel A. Consistent with the view that higher ranked BHCs in a district receive more supervisory attention, we find that BHCs ranked one through nine have on average more hours and that the 95% confidence interval for these ranks does not include zero. Again, this result is conditional on both public observables and supervisory assessments. We thus conclude that top-ranked banks receive more attention than can be explained by their size, complexity, performance, or supervisory assessments of riskiness. In terms of magnitudes, the top five banks receive almost twice as many hours as banks ranked six through nine. This suggests the one simple candidate for excess attention is a dummy variable indicating whether a bank is within the top five in its district.
Our hypothesis that higher ranked banks receive more attention does not predict a sharp discontinuity at any particular rank. In some districts, for example, the distribution of banks may be such that the sixth or seventh largest banks are similar in size to the fifth largest, in which case we would expect these banks to receive similar attention from supervisors. We therefore defineTopBHCs as those in the top five as well as those whose assets are within 25% of the assets of the fifth largest BHC in the district. Figure1, Panel B, displays these firms separately and labels them “5+.” We can see that the banks that are close in size to the fifth largest banks also receive greater supervisory attention on average and that separating these banks reduces the excess hours observed for banks ranked six through 10.12Similar results obtain when we consider a specification with district-quarter fixed effects in Internet Appendix Figure IA.1.13
To formally test whether this group ofTopBHCs receive outsized attention, we add an indicator that identifies them in equation (1). TableIIIreports the results. In this table, we only present the coefficients on our rank indicator variables, which summarizes the difference in hours these banks receive relative to our empirical benchmark model. Column (1) includes our size, complexity, and charter controls (column (1) in TableII) and column (2) considers the full set of controls for performance, asset composition, and risk rating (column (5) in TableII). The coefficient is one and statistically significant in both specifications. Moreover, the coefficient increases as we add controls, suggesting that omitted unobservable factors are not correlated with our top-rank dummy. In terms of the magnitudes, theseTopfirms receive more than twice as many supervisory hours as compared to the empirical model's predictions (for reference, a difference in log hours of 0.69 implies 100% more supervisory hours).
In columns (3) through (9), we show that our finding above is robust to alternative specifications and samples. Column (3) adds an additional dummy variable for the top 15 firms. In this specification, theTopdummy tests whether the most highly ranked BHCs are statistically different from the remaining top 15 BHCs conditional on BHC characteristics. Because the very largest BHCs in the treatment groups are not on a common size support with the untreated groups, we repeat this analysis after excluding those BHCs that are larger than the largest non-TopBHCs (column (4)). In column (5), we use the fractional polynomial in assets specification to more flexibly account for size, and in column (6), we include district-quarter fixed effects. Column (7) excludes the New York district (District 2), as this district has a unique distribution of very large banks. Lastly, columns (8) and (9) further restrict the sample to banks ranked in the top 15 with and without district-quarter fixed effects, respectively. In each of these alternative specifications, the coefficient on theTopindicator remains positive and statistically significant at the 1% level with a magnitude that suggests that top-ranked banks receive at least twice the supervisory hours as their peers. Table IA.I in the Internet Appendix confirms the robustness of these results using the simple top five rank dummy rather than ourTopindicator.
In sum, the results support the view that highly ranked banks within a district receive outsized supervisory attention. Using hours as a proxy for attention, we find that highly ranked banks receive two to three times more supervisory hours than similar BHCs.
One may be concerned that certain types of BHCs opportunistically switch districts to reduce supervisory attention. However, BHCs rarely switch districts, as this would require relocating their headquarters. Such switches generally occur in the context of cross-district mergers, where the merged entity chooses to locate its headquarters in the district of the acquired firm. During the period from 1991 to 2014, of the 353 unique BHCs that appear in the top 10 between 1991 and 2014, only five move districts (fewer than 2%).
Another possible concern is that our measure of hours does not capture the quality of supervision. Within a district, we expect the more experienced or skilled supervisors to be allocated to the largest firms. To the extent that this is the case, supervisory hours should understate the additional attention allocated to highly ranked firms. When this logic is extended across districts, supervisory talent should be clustered in districts with more banking assets. Districts with more assets contain the largest banks nationally but supply very fewTopfirms because the largest banks cannot be matched to non-Topbanks in other districts. However, these districts supply matches for the treatment group in the analysis that follows. If supervisory talent is higher in districts with more banking assets, this should bias our results against finding differences in outcomes because the additional hours spent by supervisors atTopbanks would be offset by those hours being of lower quality relative to their matches in districts with more banking assets.
article.III. Measuring the Impact of Supervision:
The empirical evidence in the prior section shows that theTopBHCs in a district receive greater supervisory attention. We next use a matching procedure to identify a sample of untreated BHCs (that is, BHCs that are not among the largest in a district and thus do not receive the “treatment” of additional supervisory attention) that are otherwise similar to the treated BHCs. We then compare outcomes across these two samples to test for the impact of supervision on bank risk and performance. By usingTopstatus to identify variation in supervisory attention, we are able to conduct our analysis over a much longer sample period, 1991 to 2014, than if we were limited to the 2006 to 2014 subperiod for which we have supervisor hours data.
To estimate the impact of greater supervisory attention, we use propensity score matching (Rosenbaum and Rubin (1983)) to construct a sample of BHCs that are not in the treatment group (i.e., not inTopgroup). We employ a matching methodology for several reasons. First, our treatment sample is naturally restricted to some of the largest, most complex BHCs. As a result, there may not be a comparable BHC in the untreated group. Matching allows us to restrict comparisons to a common support of similar BHCs. Second, a semiparametric matching procedure can better account for nonlinearities between control variables and bank outcomes, reducing our dependence on the assumption of linearity implied by ordinary least squares.
We begin with the sample of banks described in SectionII.A. Our analysis relies on the assumption that a bank's size rank within a district provides exogenous variation in supervisory attention. Hence, our matching variables are meant to control for the factors that correlate with rank but that could also impact bank risk and performance. In particular, we match on size, complexity, balance sheet characteristics, and the presence of SMB or National Bank Act chartered assets. We also match on a dummy variable indicating whether the BHC has publicly traded stock to allow for a potential role for market discipline. Finally, we match on market share—the value-weighted deposit market share of the BHC-based on Summary of Deposits data. Matching on market share ensures that we are comparing BHCs with similar market power, since market power or deposit franchise value could affect profitability and risk (Demsetz, Saidenberg, and Strahan (1996)). Each of these variables, which we define in more detail below, enters as a control in column (2) of TableII.
For robustness, we also construct an alternative matched sample that adds lagged asset growth, lagged ROA, and market-to-book (franchise value) to the set of matching variables. Our main results are qualitatively similar when these additional controls are added. However, matching on these controls reduces the sample of banks and restricts us from using these variables as outcome measures, and thus, we prefer to match on fewer characteristics in our main analysis. Results based on this sample are discussed in SectionIV.D.
The matching procedure estimates a logistic regression in each quarter, where the dependent variable is a dummy indicating whether a BHC is in the treatment sample, that is, isTopin its district, and the independent variables are our bank-level controls. We then use these estimates to calculate predicted values, or “propensity scores.” For each treatment observation, we select the two nearest neighbors based on the propensity score.14The nearest neighbors must be nontreatment observations in a different Federal Reserve district than the treatment BHC. Thus, for eachTopBHC-quarter, we have two other BHCs with similar characteristics that are not among theTopof another district. Matches are made with replacement. As a result, a BHC may appear in the control sample multiple times if it has been matched to multiple treatment observations. Our standard errors calculation explicitly accounts for repeated observations.
For this matching to succeed, the size distribution of BHCs must vary across Federal Reserve districts (see TableI). Figure2shows the geographic diversity ofTopand matched BHCs by asset size across Federal Reserve districts. Note that the geographic footprint of Federal Reserve districts does not necessarily align with that of other geographic regions. For instance, each district spans multiple states, and district borders do not necessarily conform to state borders. Consequently, state-chartered banks of BHCs in a particular Federal Reserve district may fall under the jurisdiction of different state supervisors, and banks under the jurisdiction of individual state supervisors may fall into different Federal Reserve districts. Further, theTopfirms in a district do not necessarily align with the highest size-ranked firms in a state or census region. Finally, the geographic regions of other federal supervisory agencies do not align with the 12 Federal Reserve districts.
TableIVcompares our treatment group and their matches. Over the entire sample period, we have 2,959 treatment BHC-quarters for which we are able to find two nearest neighbors on a common support. Many treatment BHCs are not matched because there are no BHCs with a similar propensity score that are untreated. In particular, the very largest BHCs are not included in the treatment sample because there are no similar BHCs in other districts that are not among theTopbanks in that district. The largest BHC in the treatment group with matches has assets of just under $100bn. The median rank of a treatment bank with a match is four.
Toe verify that these two samples are balanced, we test for differences in the matching covariates (the far right columns), where standard errors are clustered by BHC to account for correlations within BHCs over time as well as for repeat observations of matched BHCs. We find that the treatment BHCs are slightly smaller than their matches, have slightly more deposit market share, comprise fewer entities, contain more large-SMB assets, and use more deposit funding. However, none of these differences is of sizable magnitude or approaches statistical significance at standard levels. These differences are significantly smaller than using an unmatched sample, as summarized in Internet Appendix Table IA.II. The average rank of a treatment bank is 4.3, while the average rank of their match is 10.6. Hence, the difference in rank is on average 6.3. For the subset of quarters for which we have hours data, we find that on averageTopfirms receive roughly twice as many supervisory hours per quarter, a difference that is statistically significant and consistent with our findings in the linear models in TableIII, columns (5) and (6). A list of treatment banks and their matches is available upon request.
We focus on financial outcome measures related to our two main predictions: that supervisory attention reduces bank risk, and supervisory attention reduces bank performance and growth. We consider both accounting-based measures as well as market measures at supervised institutions. Accounting-based measures are constructed using quarterly regulatory filings (FR Y-9C reports). Variable definitions are detailed in Internet Appendix Section I.
On the accounting side, we employ measures of risk obtained from banks' balance sheets and income statements. With respect to the balance sheet, we use the risk-weighted assets (RWAs) of the BHC relative to total assets, the Tier 1 capital ratio (a measure of risk-weighted leverage), the percentage of loans that are nonperforming, and the ratio of loan loss reserves to total loans. Because total NPLs obscure loan mix, we also look at NPL rates within the following categories of loans: RRE, CRE, C&I, and consumer. We exclude loan category NPL percentages if the category is less than 1% of a bank's total lending.
IfTopfirms are less risky, they should have lower RWA/Assets, higher Tier 1 capital ratios, and lower NPLs. In addition, more conservative firms will have higher loan reserves given a similar NPL profile. We also consider the variation in NPLs and loan loss reserves by calculating the standard deviation over an eight-quarter-ahead horizon. Greater variation in NPLs is consistent with greater risk, whereas greater variation in loan loss reserves suggests less conservative provisioning practices. Finally, we test whether supervisors inhibit growth by examining the one-year-forward asset and loan growth of the firm.
With respect to earnings, we focus on ROA. We use the annualized level of ROA in a quarter as a measure of performance. To capture risk, we calculate the standard deviation of ROA over an eight-quarter-ahead horizon. We use a forward-looking horizon as we expect supervisory attention to affect outcomes in the future, although results are similar when we use backward-looking measures, possibly due to the persistence of treatment status. If supervision imposes costs or reduces risk-taking, we should observe a lower ROA. However, reduced risk-taking would also reduce variation in ROA. We also consider two measures that relate performance to riskiness: the Sharpe ratio of ROA and the logZ-score of the firm. We construct the Sharpe ratio as the average ROA over the next eight quarters relative to the standard deviation of ROA over that period. TheZ-score measures distance to default and is given as the number of standard deviations ROA would need to fall to wipe out book equity.15
While supervisors are concerned with risk in general, they are particularly concerned about potential failures. Unfortunately, for our econometric exercise, failures are exceptionally rare in our treatment and control groups, limiting its value as an outcome variable. We therefore generate tail risk indicators for several variables to capture whether the most extreme realizations of a particular BHC are impacted byTopstatus. The variables we consider are NPL percentage (for all loans and by loan type), the standard deviation of ROA, and ROA. We define tail events as realizations in the 5thpercentile over the full sample. For NPLs and the standard deviation of ROA, we flag tail events as those in the top 5thpercentile, whereas for ROA, we flag them in the bottom 5thpercentile.
We supplement the accounting-based measures with market prices. Accounting-based measures are subject to discretion and may lag market developments, especially for loan portfolios, which typically reflect historical book values. In contrast, market prices impound investor beliefs relatively quickly and therefore represent an important additional source of information about bank performance. In addition, because regulation is often focused on accounting measures, supervisors and supervised institutions could target accounting measures without influencing the firm's true risk as assessed by the market—market outcomes are rarely an explicit target of regulation and cannot be as easily “gamed.”
We obtain daily stock returns from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) and match to public BHCs using the New York Fed PERMCO-RSSD data set. We calculate market-to-book ratios, which measure the extent to which the current market valuation of the firm differs from its book value. Low market-to-book values may signal distress at a firm that is not yet recognized in accounting-based measures, whereas high market-to-book may signal greater anticipated future profits (or higher franchise value). We construct quarterly excess returns with respect to a standard Fama-French three-factor model (Fama and French (1993)). We also calculate daily return volatility to assess the riskiness of returns. Similar to the accounting measures, we consider return per unit of risk using Sharpe ratios. For each quarter, we scale the average daily return in excess of the risk-free rate by its standard deviation. Lastly, we consider the worst performers in any given quarter by creating an indicator for firms with excess returns in the bottom decile. Our approach to defining tail risk here differs from that for the accounting-based variables to reflect the fact that the performance measure here reflects idiosyncratic returns that are independent of the firm's exposure to Fama-French factors.
article.IV. Empirical Results:
We begin by comparing the financial measures above forTopBHCs and their matches. Assuming thatTopBHCs receive greater supervisory scrutiny but are otherwise similar to the matched sample, we attribute the differences in means between these two samples to differences in supervisory attention. We calculate standard errors that are clustered by BHC to account for correlations within BHC over time as well as repeat matches. If a treatment BHC is missing the variable of interest, then both the treatment BHC and its matched observations are excluded. The results of this analysis are reported in TableV.
Beginning with balance sheet measures of the risk-return profile of BHCs, the largest firms in a district do not have RWA/Assets or Tier 1 capital ratios that are statistically different from matched firms. However, we do find that the average percentage of NPLs is 48 bps lower forTopfirms relative to matched peers, or 25% lower relative to the matched peer sample, with the difference statistically significant at the 10% level. In addition, theTopbanks are less than half as likely to experience an NPL bank-quarter in the highest 5thpercentile, a difference that is statistically significant at the 5% level. The variability of NPLs is 25% lower (0.32% versus 0.44%) relative to the matched BHCs at significance levels of less than 5%. Thus, while these BHCs appear comparable based on RWAs and capital, the largest firms in a district have higher quality loans whose performance is less volatile.
When we consider NPLs by loan type, we find thatTopBHCs have lower levels of NPLs for all but consumer loans (the difference is statistically significant at the 5% level for C&I and at the 11% and 12% levels for RRE and CRE, respectively). Despite having safer loans on average, loan loss reserves atTopBHCs are not significantly different from their matches, suggesting that they are more conservative than their peers. Finally,TopBHCs grow slightly faster than their matches, although the differences are not statistically significant, and these BHCs are less likely to have asset growth in the lowest 5% tail, suggesting that they achieve this risk profile without sacrificing overall asset or loan growth.
Warnings also suggests that theTopBHCs in a district are less risky with little trade-off in terms of profitability. While the level of ROA is similar betweenTopBHCs and their matches, the standard deviation of ROA forTopBHCs is 60% that of their peers, with the difference significant at the 5% level. In addition, treated firms are half as likely to appear in the highest 5thpercentile, with this difference having ap-value of roughly 10%.
Given that returns are similar but volatility is lower, it is not surprising that the accounting Sharpe ratio (SD ROA/ROA) is greater for theTopBHCs. Similarly,Z-scores atTopBHCs are significantly higher than those at matched firms, suggesting that these BHCs hold higher amounts of capital relative to the riskiness of their earnings streams and therefore are less likely to default. Note, however, that the actual capital ratios between the two sets of firms are similar. The difference is driven primarily by the standard deviation of ROA. Overall, the results show that top-ranked BHCs that are subject to more intense supervision have a better risk-return trade-off than lower size-ranked institutions.
The market-based measures echo these results, albeit at lower levels of statistical significance. In particular,TopBHCs are less likely to appear in the bottom decile of returns (p-value of 12%), while average excess returns, the Sharpe ratio, and the market-to-book ratio are higher. For these measures, inference is based on a smaller sample as approximately 10% of our matched sample is not publicly traded.
Taken together, the results suggest thatTopBHCs—those subject to greater supervisory attention—are less risky and enjoy a better risk-return trade-off than otherwise similar BHCs not among theTopranked in their district. These results are stronger for accounting-based measures than for market-based measures.
The results in TableVsuggest thatTopfirms experience less volatile earnings and better loan performance—consistent with the view that supervisory attention reduces risk—but with little evidence of a trade-off in terms of profitability or growth. To better understand the timing of these differences across the business cycle, we compare the time series of the performance for the treated and control banks over the 1991 to 2014 sample period. Figure3plots sample averages over time ofTopbanks and their matches for ROA, SD ROA, and NPL percentages.
Patterns in ROA betweenTopbanks and their matches are similar in Figure3, Panel A, consistent with the results in in TableV. Differences between the two samples emerge in the remaining panels of the figure. The standard deviation of ROA, as shown in Figure3, Panel B, suggests thatTopbanks are less risky around three recessionary periods: the early 1990s, 2001, and the Great Recession. Similar patterns emerge for NPLs (Panel C), particularly RRE during the crisis (Panel D), CRE in the early 1990s and the crisis (Panel E), and C&I during all three recessions (Panel F). We do not show consumer loans as we do not detect differential performance in this category. WhileTopbanks vary in their sensitivity to a downturn by loan type, in general, we find thatTopbanks are less sensitive to systematic declines in loan performance.
The graphical evidence suggests that there is a cyclical nature to the benefits of supervisory attention: in normal times, differences are smaller, but during downturns, the more closely supervised firms exhibit better loan performance and lower earnings volatility. Of course, because the patterns illustrated in Figure3may not be statistically significant, we use a regression framework to test for the differential sensitivity of profitability and risk during periods of industry stress. The pooled cross-sectional regression uses the sample ofTopBHCs-quarters and their matches. We regress our profitability and risk variables on an indicator forTopstatus, an indicator for an industry downturn, and an interaction term between the two. The coefficient on the interaction term indicates the differential sensitivity ofTopBHCs to the downturn relative to normal times.
Defining an industry downturn based on periods in which banks experience high losses, rather than on official recession dates, is key to the results. We define downturns using industry aggregates for our outcome variables of interest. A downturn quarter is determined by whether a symmetric five-quarter moving window has at least three quarters that are above (or below in the case of ROA) the median for the entire sample period. The moving window eliminates higher frequency switching between states and creates downturn indicators that accord with periods of industry distress in the specific variable of interest. Internet Appendix Figure IA.2 shows downturn periods by variable. Our results are qualitatively similar if we instead use an indicator for realizations above the median. We do not use official recession timing to identify downturns because bank outcomes, such as losses and NPLs, are realized with a lag and remain elevated well after a recession is over.
TableVIsummarizes the regression results. With respect to profitability (ROA), we find thatTopBHCs earn more than their matches during downturns, but the coefficient is not statistically significant. Unconditionally,Topfirms have significantly less variation in ROA during good times and this magnitude is even greater during downturns, although thep-value on the interaction coefficient is only 0.16. For NPLs, we find thatTopBHCs have lower NPL percentages in downturns. This is true for total loans, reflecting lower NPLs in RRE, CRE, and C&I lending, and is statistically significant in each of these categories. Hence, the regression analysis supports the statistical significance of the patterns observed in Figure3: supervisory attention appears to reduce bank risk, particularly during industry downturns. The remainder of the paper explores these relationships further using an econometric approach that accounts for differences across districts over time.
A key limitation of the difference-in-means analysis is that we compare BHCs across Federal Reserve districts. While the BHCs in our sample have geographically diverse operations, if there are unobserved district-level effects and our sample of treatment and controls is unbalanced across districts, then our results may be biased. For example, those districts with smallerTopbanks might experience less economic volatility and be less exposed to systematic risk than those districts with largeTopbanks that tend to populate the control sample.
To account for time-varying district-level differences, we construct a larger sample of BHCs that allows us to specify an empirical model that includes district-quarter fixed effects. Specifically, we augment our matched sample by matching non-TopBHCs of size rank 6 through 15 to banks not among theTopof another district, where matches are based on the same propensity score matching approach described in SectionII.A. Hence, the sample grows to include each top 15 bank that we can match to two other banks in another district. This analysis allows us to include district-quarter fixed effects that capture average differences in the performance by district both forTopbanks' districts and for matched banks' districts. Internet Appendix Table IA.III shows that there are not significant differences between top 15 banks and their matches.
The results reported in TableVIIare broadly consistent with the difference-in-means results in TableV.TopBHCs continue to have less volatile NPLs and are less likely to appear in the top tail of NPL realizations at the 1% and 10% statistical significance levels, respectively. They also have lower NPLs on average, although the coefficient has ap-value of 16%. As with the simple matching approach,Topfirms are less likely to have a negative asset growth outcome, consistent with a lower likelihood of severe distress. We do not find a statistically significant difference in asset or loan growth atTopBHCs, although on average they grow 50 to 60 bps slower. As before, we cannot conclude that lower risk comes at the expense of credit provision.
When we turn to loan types,Topbanks have lower NPLs at the 10% significance level for RRE and CRE loans. The only category of loans for whichTopbanks do not have lower NPLs is consumer loans. This may reflect the fact that consumer lending is a credit-score-driven business, with less of a role for supervisory insights or soft information, or it may indicate that we are not correctly accounting for subcategories of consumer lending such as credit cards and auto loans. Similar to TableV,TopBHCs have less volatile ROA, a higher accounting Sharpe ratio, and a higher Z-score, each of which is significant at the 5% level.
The market-based results are considerably stronger in these specifications relative to TableV, withTopBHCs having less volatile daily returns and a smaller probability of being in the lowest decile of returns at the 1% and 10% significance levels, respectively. The lower risk is not accompanied by a performance trade-off, asTopBHCs display similar Sharpe ratios and significantly higher market-to-book ratios. Thus, the lower risk we observe in accounting returns is mirrored in market price data, suggesting that increased supervisory attention is associated with less risky firms and not driven by differences across districts. Internet Appendix Table IA.IV shows that these results are robust to excluding New York district matches, and Internet Appendix Table IA.V shows that the cyclical variation using this sample and method is qualitatively similar to the findings summarized in TableVI.
While our primary matching specification conditions on a number of variables that could be associated with rank as well as bank performance,Topstatus is not randomly assigned. Thus, there may be other unobservable heterogeneity that explains the stronger performance ofTopbanks.
Some examples of unobserved heterogeneity that might result in omitted variable bias are managerial talent, firm culture, or franchise value. With respect to managerial talent, if there is a national market for banking management talent, this should be accounted for in our size match, for example, when CEO talent is sorted by firm size (Gabaix and Landier (2008)). If, instead, the market for talent is based on local market share, then since we also match on that characteristic,Topbanks and their matches should have similar appeal in attracting managerial talent. Similarly, to the extent that market share is indicative of franchise value, we already control for management sorting along that dimension.
Another way to address unobserved heterogeneity would be to include firm-level fixed effects to exploit entry and exit fromTopranked status or to conduct an event study around changes in status. However, such an analysis faces several challenges. First,Topstatus changes occur infrequently (96% of BHCs that are in theTopin a district remain in theTopin the next quarter) and tend to involve a small set of firms, and thus tests based on status changes lack power. Second, entry and exit is likely to be endogenous to firm performance. Firms that enter (leave) theTopare likely to be growing faster (slower) than their peers in the same district, and firms that enter theTopdue to an acquisition of a large firm by an out-of-district firm may be facing a change in the competitive environment. Third, we do not have a strong prior on the time that it takes for increased supervisory attention to result in different outcomes. As observed in Figure3, the differences in risk concentrate in industry downturns, and thus, it may be years before entry or exit is reflected in bank performance. This biases against finding results both in our main analysis and in an event study context, where the effect of supervisory attention is unlikely to be instantaneous.
While we cannot exploit within-firm variation, we can consider an expanded set of matching criteria that include recent firm performance using the latest 12 months ROA, asset growth, and the franchise value of the firm as measured by market-to-book. Recent profitability and asset growth should capture heterogeneity in firm profitability and growth, whereas franchise value captures the market's expectations of future earnings relative to book equity. These metrics can proxy for managerial talent (ROA, market-to-book, growth), expectations of future growth (market-to-book), and franchise value (market-to-book). While the sample size drops due to the more stringent matching criteria and the restriction to public firms (see Internet Appendix Table IA.VI), the results in Internet Appendix Table IA.VII (which include district-quarter fixed effects) are consistent with our findings in TablesVandVII:Topfirms exhibit lower NPLs but similar loan loss reserves and are less volatile, even when we consider market measures. The robustness of our results extends to the cyclicality results, as summarized in Internet Appendix Table IA.VIII. Thus, controlling for recent performance and franchise value does not change our earlier findings, alleviating concerns related to potential omitted variables.
article.V. Additional Analyses:
In this section, we further explore the nature of the differences in earnings volatility and other risks betweenTopfirms and their matches using the district-quarter fixed effects specification of the model. We also implement a two-stage least squares (2SLS) specification that is restricted to the 2006 to 2014 period.
One of our more robust results is the finding thatTopBHCs have lower earnings volatility than otherwise comparable BHCs that are not among the largest in their districts. In this section, we explore this finding in more detail to identify the sources of lower earnings volatility forTopBHCs. To do so, we decompose net income (the numerator of ROA) into four key components: net interest margin (NIM), noninterest income such as fees and trading revenue, loan loss provisions (LLPs), and other noninterest expenses (noninterest expenses excluding compensation and fixed asset expenses).16Each of these is scaled by total assets. We calculate the standard deviation of each of the resulting ratios over an eight-quarter-forward horizon. Using these outcome variables, we repeat the analysis of differences betweenTopBHCs and the matched sample, controlling for district-quarter effects (as in TableVII). These results are reported in the top four rows of TableVIII.
Consistent with the overall results for the volatility of ROA, the volatility of each of these key net components is lower forTopBHCs than for the matched sample. The differences are statistically significant for NIM, noninterest income, and LLPs, though not for other noninterest expenses. The lower volatility of provisions is consistent with the finding thatTopBHCs have less volatile NPLs and supports the view that these firms hold less risky loan portfolios and are more conservative over time. The lower volatility of net interest income might also reflect this finding to some extent, as more stable loan portfolios can generate less volatile interest income on loans and could reflect better management of interest rate risk. The lower volatility of noninterest income atTopBHCs suggests that the impact of supervision extends beyond the effect on BHCs' lending to other activities at the firm. The pervasive nature of the lower volatility is consistent with efforts on the part of supervisors to foster better governance and risk management throughout the bank.
In addition to examining reported income and expenses, we calculate a series of variables intended to isolate the discretionary portions of net income. Firms have discretion over the recognition of some parts of income and expenses, to the extent that these components rely on models or management judgment. These areas include the timing of LLPs and net charge-offs, the timing of losses on securities held in the available-for-sale portfolio, and reserves for events such as legal settlements (part of noninterest expenses). For this reason, we try to distinguish between decreased volatility that arises from lower risk and decreased volatility that reflects earnings management.
Following prior accounting literature (e.g., Moyer (1990)), we estimate discretionary accounting behavior using deviations from predicted values for LLPs and realized security gains. We estimate discretionary behavior in the broadest sample of above-median asset size BHCs and estimate deviations as follows. For LLPs, we regress changes in the ratio of LLPs to average loans held in a quarter on changes in the ratio of NPLs to loans, the change in net charge-offs to loans, the level of loan loss reserves to total loans, and district-quarter fixed effects. Discretionary LLPs are the residuals from this regression, and thus, they capture the deviation from the time-period average after adjusting for the firm's loss experiences. Similarly, for security gains, we regress quarterly realized security gains/losses scaled by assets on unrealized security gains in the available-for-sale portfolio scaled by assets and time fixed effects. We focus on the absolute value of these residuals to capture the discretionary activity. Lastly, we estimate total discretionary earnings as discretionary security gains less discretionary LLPs scaled by assets. The results for the discretionary measures are reported in the bottom four rows of TableVIII.
TopBHCs exhibit significantly less discretionary behavior for both LLPs and discretionary securities gains. The overall impact on earnings is negative, but the difference in earnings is not statistically significant. Hence, top size-ranked firms are less likely to deviate from typical provision levels given their experience. This finding is consistent with the idea that BHCs subject to greater supervisory attention take a more conservative approach to loan loss reserves.
While we find evidence of lower accounting measures of risk, one may be concerned that banks substitute for other risks that are more difficult to observe. The market-based measures such as stock price volatility suggest that market participants do not perceive an increase in other risks. We also find decreased volatility in both ROA and noninterest income, accounting measures that might be correlated with off-balance-sheet risks. Notwithstanding, we run additional analysis to further understand if banks that receive more supervisory attention are increasing other risks that may not be captured by the accounting and market measures.
First, we look for measures of off-balance-sheet activity. Unfortunately, many measures of off-balance-sheet activity were added to regulatory reports only after 2009, so we examine only the subset of measures that are available over our full sample period. Results of regressions on the matched sample including district-quarter fixed effects are reported in the top panel of TableIX. We look at unused loan commitments, securitization income, and noninterest income, all normalized by assets. These measures should capture activities for which income is not as closely linked to balance-sheet activities. We find thatTopBHCs do not earn more net securitization income, nor do they have more noninterest income. In contrast, we find thatTopBHCs have higher amounts of unused loan commitments. This result is suggestive of additional liquidity risk, since these firms have similar ratios of loans to assets. However, this result is also consistent with the idea that more supervisory attention does not reduce intermediation, since loan commitments are a critical channel of credit supply.
In addition to these financial measures of risk, we look at nonfinancial measures that may relate to firm risk. First, we examine the governance structure of the firm, particularly as it relates to risk management. We also look at supervisory actions. Greater scrutiny, all else equal, may increase the degree to which supervisors use ratings and enforcement actions to influence a bank's behavior. However, it is also possible that increased supervisory attention means that firm behavior may be influenced without need for more formal actions.
While there is a wealth of data on BHCs' financials, information on internal governance is not as easy to measure over a long time horizon. We capture the importance of risk management at a BHC by using a dummy variable that indicates whether the BHC has a Risk Committee or a Chief Risk Officer (CRO). To do so, we match public BHCs to their proxy filings. We then conduct text searches on these filings. If the filing mentions a Risk Committee or a CRO, we set the relevant indicator variable to 1.17CROs are extremely rare in filings prior to 2006, and thus, we use this indicator only from 2006 onward. Our assumption is that a BHC with a Risk Committee or a CRO places more managerial focus on the importance of monitoring and mitigating risk. Using our proxies for the existence of a Risk Committee or a CRO, we test whether supervisory attention results in better risk governance.
The last category of comparison is supervisory tools. We are able to measure several tools that supervisors use to influence BHC behavior. The first are MRAs and MRIAs. As described previously, MRAs and MRIAs are supervisory actions intended to ensure that firms remediate unsafe or unsound practices or conditions and regulatory violations. MRAs and MRIAs are by far the most common type of supervisory action (Eisenbach et al. (2017)) and are assigned to banks much earlier than public enforcement actions. We construct variables for the number of open MRAs and MRIAs at the end of each quarter, as well as the number of new MRAs and MRIAs generated by supervisors during the quarter. These data are available over the period from 2009Q4 to 2014Q4.
The third tool we use is BHC supervisory ratings. As described above, supervisors assign composite ratings to BHCs that reflect the overall extent of concerns about the institution. A rating of “1” corresponds to the lowest level of concern, while “5” corresponds to the highest.18A high rating can result in restrictions on BHC activities, including but not limited to the acquisition of another institution or expansion into new activities. We examine the level of the rating to see if supervisory attention leads to lower ratings, as well as the frequency of ratings changes, as more supervisory attention might lead to greater use of this tool to influence bank behavior.
The results are reported in TableIX. We find no significant differences in the governance measures or most supervisory measures betweenTopBHCs and other firms. Thus, while supervisors are devoting more attention to these firms, this extra attention does not result in more supervisory actions. That being said, given that these firms are, in fact, less risky, it may be the case that conditional on risk, more closely supervised firms receive more MRAs and MRIAs. Also, about half of an additional MRA/MRIA is closed atTopBHCs per quarter, with the difference having ap-value of 0.11, which is consistent with the idea that increased supervisory attention might allow BHCs and supervisors to address supervisory concerns more quickly.
Our underlying identification assumption—that, all else equal, the top-ranked firms in Federal Reserve districts receive more attention—can be applied in an alternative empirical framework.
