Specifically, rather than estimating reduced-form estimates based on theTopdummy, we instrument for log hours using 2SLS. This methodology comes at a cost, as we are restricted to the smaller sample period for which we have hours, 2006 to 2014. However, it also allows us to verify our results using an alternative estimation procedure and to quantify changes in bank outcomes in terms of our proxy for attention, supervisory hours.
An uninstrumented regression of outcomes on hours (Internet Appendix Table IA.IX) reveals that more risky firms receive more supervisory hours. Hours are positively associated with NPLs, volatility, and supervisory issues. Firms that grow more slowly have lower ROA, or lower market returns also receive more supervisory hours. This suggests that our matching exercise, as well as the 2SLS results discussed below, help ameliorate the underlying identification problem that risky, poorly performing firms receive more supervisory attention.
TableXpresents results of 2SLS estimations for the primary financial and nonfinancial outcome measures outlined earlier in this section. Similar to the analysis on hours, we begin with a sample of all BHCs with above-median size and exclude those that are larger than the largest untreated bank to minimize the impact of extremely large banks that are not on a common support (as in TableIII, columns (4) to (6)); the findings are similar if we include the largest firms. We instrument for supervisory hours withTop, and we use the same controls as in TableII, column (2), which are also the same controls as those used in our primary matching specification (e.g., TableIV). TheF-statistics, which are reported in the third column of TableX, satisfy standard tests for weak instruments (all greater than 10).
Results of the 2SLS analysis are similar to those in TablesVandVII, although with somewhat lower statistical significance, reflecting lower statistical power from the shorter time series. A 20% increase in supervisory hours is associated with a 14bp lower NPL rate, a reduction of 5% relative to the mean. Increased supervisory hours are also associated with significantly less volatile ROA, which results in higher accounting Sharpe ratios and higherZ-scores. Moreover, a 20% increase in hours reduces the volatility of BHC earnings by 7 bps, a reduction of 9% relative to the mean. Increased supervisory hours are also associated with lower standard deviation of returns, but these results are not statistically significant in this time period. In this specification, we find that higher hours are associated with lower Tier 1 ratios, suggesting that more supervised firms hold less Tier 1 capital. This result was not robust to the various other specifications in the paper. The findings are qualitatively similar if we include district-quarter fixed effects (see Internet Appendix Table IA.X).
The 2SLS results lend further support to our findings in the longer time period, in terms of both statistical significance and directional impact. However, the magnitudes should be interpreted with caution. First, while we believe that hours data are a useful proxy for supervisory attention, hours data fail to capture information on the quality of hours. Our prior is that quality and quantity of supervisory hours are positively related, but the reverse could attenuate our estimated coefficients. In addition, the hours data are available only for 2006 onward, which limits our statistical power. This time period is also one that is particularly volatile for financial performance.
Each of the empirical specifications above implies a relationship between supervisory attention and bank outcomes. While our measure of hours is only a proxy for supervisory attention, we can relate hours to differences in outcomes in an attempt to quantify the local average treatment effect of additional hours on bank risk. For instance, a 20% increase in hours results in a 9% reduction in volatility based on the 2SLS results in TableX. If we take the incremental hours for top-ranked banks in the 2006 to 2014 portion of the sample (top-ranked banks have roughly two times the hours) and apply it to the matched differences for the longer time period (TablesVandVII), the implications are similar—a 20% difference in hours suggests a 9% to 10% decline in earnings volatility.19
One implication of the distance to default statistic (Z-score) is that we can calculate how much additional capital a non-Topbank would need to have the same distance to default as aTopbank that receives more supervisory hours. Based on the 2SLS results, the log difference inZ-scores for a 20% change in hours is roughly 0.08, which equates to aZ-score difference in levels of 2.3. Assuming the sample average for the 2SLS ROA, 0.66, and the standard deviation of ROA, 0.76, this result implies that a bank would need a 1.6% higher equity-to-asset ratio to close this gap. A similar exercise using the matched sample results in TablesVandVIIsuggests a capital ratio difference of 1.4% to 1.8%.20
While we do not intend for these estimates to suggest that simply raising hours by 20% can account for a lower level of capital, they do suggest that the differences we find are sizable and that an emphasis on supervision can make firms more safe on a level that is significant relative to the capital levels of these institutions.
article.VI. Conclusions:
In this paper, we exploit the geographic assignment of supervisory responsibilities in the Federal Reserve System to examine the impact of supervision. We demonstrate that the largest BHCs in a Federal Reserve district receive greater supervisory attention in the form of more dedicated supervisory hours, even after controlling for factors such as the size, complexity, and market share of the institution. Leveraging this result, we compare top size-ranked banks within a district to similar banks in other districts to assess the impact of greater supervisory attention. Our results are consistent with increased supervisory attention resulting in lower bank risk as indicated by less risky lending, lower earnings volatility, and more conservative accounting practices. Given that our sample of BHCs is subject to similar regulatory regimes, our findings provide novel evidence that supervision matters. The magnitudes we document suggest a role for supervision to reduce banking losses in downturns and to help similarly capitalized banks avoid default.
Our findings are subject to a few important caveats. First, as with any natural experiment, if there are unobserved determinants ofTopstatus and bank performance, then we may be misattributing the improvements in bank performance to supervision. Second, we do not attempt to measure the social welfare benefits of supervision in this paper, as such an analysis would require a comprehensive measures of the costs of supervision and the benefits of financial stability. That said, at current levels of supervision, we do not find a trade-off between the provision of credit and financial stability. This is consistent with our finding that the risk-return trade-off appears better at more closely supervised firms.
Third, our objective to capture the broad benefits of supervisory attention limits our ability to identify the specific mechanisms through which supervisors accomplish these goals. The improved risk-return frontier might be explained by the presence of supervisors resolving governance problems within firms, effectively improving their overall performance. This could arise through increased power of risk managers or through increased board engagement spurred by contact with supervisors. To explore these avenues further would require more detailed information about supervisors' communications and the costs of supervisors' time. Some information of this type is available in confidential Federal Reserve supervisory data (e.g., from systems intended to assist management of the supervisory areas of the Federal Reserve). Our hope is to explore these data in future work.
